text.title: Fine-Tuning LoRA: Technique Comparison and Selection
text.summary: LoRA, QLoRA, DoRA, and rsLoRA comparison; hardware requirements; rank and scaling factor selection rules.

section.title: How LoRA Works
section.p: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) freezes the original weights W and trains two small matrices B and A such that the adapted weights W' = W + BA. Rank r controls the size of B (d×r) and A (r×k). At inference, the delta BA is merged into W — no extra latency.

rule.statement: Use r=16, alpha=32 as starting defaults for most fine-tuning tasks. This provides stable training dynamics (alpha/r=2 scaling) without overfitting on small datasets.
rule.rationale: r=16 captures enough task-specific signal for most NLP tasks. Setting alpha=2r scales the LoRA output appropriately — values much lower than 1 cause the adapter to underfit; values much higher cause instability. Empirically, r=16/alpha=32 is the most robust starting point before task-specific tuning.

rule.statement: Target modules: always include q_proj and v_proj. For better quality add k_proj, o_proj, and gate_proj/up_proj/down_proj (MLP). Never add lm_head — it causes catastrophic output format corruption.
rule.rationale: Attention projection matrices carry the most task-specific information. Adding MLP projections improves quality at the cost of more trainable parameters. The lm_head controls token probabilities directly — fine-tuning it frequently breaks output formatting and requires much more data.

rule.statement: Use QLoRA (4-bit NF4 quantization + LoRA) when GPU VRAM is under 24GB. Use full LoRA (bf16) when VRAM allows. Use DoRA when initial fine-tuning quality is below baseline with standard LoRA. Use rsLoRA when r > 32.
rule.rationale: QLoRA enables 70B model fine-tuning on a single A100 80GB or two 24GB GPUs. DoRA (Weight-Decomposed LoRA) decomposes updates into magnitude and direction components, outperforming LoRA on low-data regimes at the cost of ~10% more compute. rsLoRA scales the LoRA output by 1/sqrt(r) rather than 1/r, stabilizing training when using high ranks.

list.item: LoRA: bf16, r=16/alpha=32, no quantization; requires full VRAM for base model; fastest training
QLoRA: 4-bit NF4 base + bf16 adapter; 60-70% VRAM reduction; 10-20% slower training; minimal quality loss
DoRA: decomposes magnitude+direction; better on <500 examples; 10% more compute; use when LoRA underperforms baseline
rsLoRA: 1/sqrt(r) scaling; use when r > 32; prevents gradient vanishing at high ranks

example.code:
from peft import LoraConfig, TaskType
# Standard LoRA defaults lora_config = LoraConfig( task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM, r=16, lora_alpha=32, lora_dropout=0.05, target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj", "k_proj", "o_proj"], bias="none", )
# QLoRA: add BitsAndBytesConfig to model loading from transformers import BitsAndBytesConfig bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig( load_in_4bit=True, bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4", bnb_4bit_compute_dtype="bfloat16", bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True, )

list.item: 70B model, QLoRA: 2× A100 80GB (tensor parallel) or 4× A100 40GB

rule.statement: Enable gradient checkpointing and set per_device_train_batch_size=1 with gradient_accumulation_steps=4-8 when VRAM is tight. This simulates batch size 4-8 without storing all intermediate activations simultaneously.
rule.rationale: Gradient checkpointing recomputes intermediate activations during backprop instead of storing them, trading compute for memory. For LoRA fine-tuning, the dominant VRAM consumer is activations — checkpointing can free 40-60% of activation VRAM at ~20% throughput cost.

rule.statement: Set r by task complexity: r=4 for style/format tasks, r=8 for domain adaptation, r=16 for instruction following, r=32-64 for large behavioral shifts. Validate with eval: if loss plateau occurs early, increase r; if overfitting, decrease r.
rule.rationale: Rank is the bottleneck of representational capacity for the adapter. Style tasks require minimal new representations; behavioral rewrites require higher rank. Going beyond r=64 rarely helps for most tasks and increases parameter count significantly.
